game_name: Heroes of Might and Magic II

danger_signals.visual_form: Opposing faction town structures on adventure map
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring military engagement or avoidance

danger_signals.visual_form: Stacks of enemy units visible on adventure map
danger_signals.meaning: Mobile threat that can initiate combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Neutral creature dwellings and resource guardians on map
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazards blocking progression or draining resources

safety_signals.visual_form: Player-colored town structure with upgraded buildings
safety_signals.meaning: Safe haven for hero recovery and resource generation
Faction partner in campaign; non-hostile in multi-faction good/evil scenarios

safety_signals.visual_form: Mine, forest, and artifact locations on adventure map
safety_signals.meaning: Strategic positions for economic power; accessible without combat threat

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Medieval castle towns with towers and fortifications
Rugged, fortress-like settlements with war-camp aesthetics
Natural, mystical architecture integrated with forests
Dark, corrupted settlements with arcane structures
Towers and magical academies with ethereal aesthetics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Knight, Sorceress, and Wizard town colors and structures predominate on map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player controls three aligned factions; AI behavior follows good-aligned strategy

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Barbarian, Warlock, and Necromancer town colors and dark aesthetics predominate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player controls three evil-aligned factions; darker narrative tone and enemy behaviors

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Divided castle ownership between Archibald and Roland forces; map shows contested territories
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Campaign missions require conquest of enemy strongholds; hero armies clash over strategic points

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Town structures expand from basic castle to fully upgraded castle with visible building additions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Upgraded castles produce stronger unit stacks and grant access to higher-tier creatures

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hero unit sprite and accompanying army stack size grow visually with experience and gathered forces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Veterans can engage multiple enemies; strength visually represented by army diversity and number

environmental_storytelling.example: Knight towns feature orderly, fortified medieval castles; Necromancer towns display graveyards and ruins; Wizard towers show arcane aesthetics. This communicates faction identity and alignment without explicit labels.
Scenarios progress through territorial control, showing how Archibald and Roland's conflict unfolds across Enroth. Map state at scenario start and victory conditions tell the story of succession war.
Placement of gold mines, forests, and creature dwellings creates natural chokepoints and strategic corridors, implying contested lands and valuable territories worth fighting over.
Different hero sprites represent class and faction; secondary skills gained over time are tracked in UI, showing hero growth and specialization narrative.
Town colors immediately signal faction identity and player vs. enemy ownership. Map readability depends on consistent color-to-faction mapping.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves predictably; indicates mobile threat that will engage if path crosses player hero
Initiates combat if player attempts to claim guarded object; strength scales with strategic importance of resource
Immediately switches color to player faction; begins generating resources and recruitable units for player side
Visual stack representation grows more diverse and visually impressive with better troops; signals combat power without explicit stats shown
Scenario transitions to next map; narrative progresses based on good/evil campaign branch taken

notes: Heroes II uses color-coded faction identity and town architecture as primary visual communication tools. The good/evil alignment split (3 good factions: Knight, Sorceress, Wizard; 3 evil factions: Barbarian, Warlock, Necromancer) is reinforced through campaign structure, map control, and architectural theme. World state is communicated through adventure map composition—which factions control which territories, resource distribution, and hero positioning. Political succession narrative (Archibald vs. Roland) is embedded in scenario progression rather than explicit visual markers. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on faction-specific aesthetic themes and strategic map layout.
